name: ichiba.library.fiction.sample
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Scarlet Path, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

name: red.greave.1
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Scarlet Path, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

Red Greave did not turn when the door closed behind him.

He knew the sound of fear too well to mistake it for anything else, and the man standing there had fear clinging to him like damp cloth.

"You can speak," Red Greave said, calmly. He rested one gauntleted hand on the pommel of his sword, not as a threat, but as a reminder - to himself as much as to the other man - that some things in this world could still be relied upon.

The room was small. Poorly kept. Ichiban cheap-lodging small. The kind of place where hopes were unpacked once and never properly folded again.

"They say you never hesitate," the man said at last.

Red Greave smiled, faintly.

"They are wrong."

He always hesitated. Just long enough to remember the cost.

The light caught the edge of his blade as he drew it free, steel whispering like a secret told only once. The sword was not angry. It was patient. It had waited longer than either of them.

"You should leave this city," Red Greave continued, his voice low. "Before the Maze notices you."

The man blinked.

"The Maze doesn't notice people."

Red Greave finally turned then, and for just a heartbeat, the certainty left his eyes.

"That," he said softly, "is what everyone believes."

Outside, the city went on breathing. Somewhere, a press clattered. Somewhere else, a door opened that should not have had a handle at all.

Red Greave stepped forward.

And this time, he did not hesitate.

name: red.greave.2
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Blade of Kharos, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The blade came out of the dark fast enough to steal the breath from the men watching.

Red Greave caught it on steel, sparks snapping like angry insects between them. The alley was too narrow for footwork, too slick with old rain and worse things. Someone laughed from a window above. Someone always did.

"You are not paid enough for this," Red Greave said, shifting his grip.

The man with the Blade of Kharos snarled and pressed forward. The sword was wrong in the hand, its edge too eager, drinking the light instead of reflecting it. Red Greave felt it pull, just slightly, as if it recognized him.

He did not let it.

The clash rang again. Once. Twice. On the third strike, Red Greave stepped inside the arc and drove his pommel into the man's ribs. There was a crack like breaking wood. The sword fell. The alley went quiet.

Red Greave stood still, listening.

No cheering. No grateful witnesses. Just the city breathing around him, patient and unkind.

He wiped his blade clean on his sleeve, then nudged the fallen sword away with his boot.

"Find another hand," he told it.

The blade did not answer.

Red Greave turned his back on it anyway.

name: red.greave.3
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Crown of Ashes, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The crown lay where the fire had died.

It was smaller than the stories said it would be. Most dangerous things were.

Red Greave stopped at the edge of the chamber, boots sinking slightly into warm ash. The ruin breathed around him, stone ticking as it cooled, old heat retreating inch by inch. Somewhere deeper below, something shifted and went still again.

"No," he said, quietly, to no one at all.

The Crown of Ashes rested on a low plinth of blackened stone. It was cracked down one side, its jewels dulled, its power leaking out like smoke that refused to rise. A lesser hero would have rushed it. A wiser one would have turned away.

Red Greave advanced slowly.

He remembered the warning carved into the gate outside. He remembered the man who had paid him to come here, and the way that man had avoided saying why.

Red Greave lifted the crown with both hands.

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the air grew heavy, as if the ruin itself were holding its breath. He felt the weight of choices not yet made settle onto his shoulders, hotter than any flame.

Red Greave lowered the crown back onto the stone.

"Not today," he said.

The ruin exhaled. Somewhere far away, something that had been waiting grew impatient.

Red Greave turned and walked out, leaving the crown where it was meant to remain.

The ash closed behind him, quietly, like a door.

name: red.greave.4
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Sorcerer of Black Fen, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The mist reached his knees before the water did.

The Black Fen had a way of swallowing distance. Trees became shadows. Shadows became memories. Even the sound of his own footsteps seemed reluctant to follow.

A voice drifted across the reeds.

"You have come farther than most."

Red Greave stopped beside the broken causeway and rested one hand upon the pommel of his sword.

"Then let my journey not be wasted," he answered.

The mist parted just enough to reveal a tall figure standing upon the water itself, cloaked in robes as black as the fen below. A crooked staff rested in one hand, its head bound with roots that still lived.

"You seek the Sorcerer of Black Fen?"

"I seek the man who has forgotten the difference between power and wisdom."

The sorcerer smiled.

"Bold words. Do you know how many have spoken them?"

Red Greave drew his sword, the steel bright against the grey morning.

"Many have spoken," he said. "Too few have acted."

The waters stirred.

Faces appeared beneath the surface, pale and silent, drifting just below the reeds. They opened their eyes as one.

The sorcerer raised his staff.

"This fen remembers every soul it has claimed."

Red Greave took another step onto the flooded stones.

"Then let it remember one more thing."

"And what is that?"

Red Greave lifted his blade until its point was level with the sorcerer's heart.

"That no darkness is so deep that a single man cannot choose to walk into it."

The reeds bent though there was no wind.

Then the water began to rise.

name: red.greave.5
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Warlord of Iron Reach, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

Smoke rolled across the foundry floor, carrying the scent of hot iron and burned oil. Hammers lay abandoned where the smiths had fled, their forges still roaring in the gloom.

Red Greave walked alone between them.

"Show yourself," he called. "A ruler who hides behind his walls has already surrendered them."

Iron doors groaned open at the far end of the hall.

The Warlord of Iron Reach emerged in blackened plate, a great hammer slung across one shoulder. Sparks danced from every step he took.

"You have courage," the warlord said. "But courage does not break iron."

Red Greave smiled.

"No. Men break iron. Courage merely teaches them to begin."

With a roar, the warlord swung the hammer in a glittering arc. The blow shattered an anvil where Red Greave had stood only a heartbeat before.

Red Greave's sword flashed in reply, carving a bright line across the warlord's breastplate.

Steel rang through the foundry.

"Hear me," Red Greave declared, circling his foe. "You have forged chains and called them laws. You have forged weapons and called them peace. Today you shall learn that neither iron nor fear can hold a people forever."

The warlord laughed, though his laughter carried less certainty than before.

"You speak as though words can win battles."

Red Greave raised his blade until it caught the furnace light.

"Words do not win them," he said. "They decide whether they are worth fighting."

The furnaces roared higher.

Somewhere above, a great chain snapped.

Neither man looked away.

name: red.greave.6
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Siege of Blackwall, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The bells began before sunrise.

Red Greave stood upon the western wall as the mist lifted from the valley below, revealing a forest of spears where fields had stood the day before.

The captain joined him, pale with exhaustion.

"There are too many."

Red Greave rested his hands upon the battlements.

"There are always too many."

The first ram rolled into view, its iron head fashioned in the likeness of a snarling wolf.

"Open the gates," cried a herald below. "Yield, and Blackwall shall be spared."

Red Greave leaned over the parapet.

"You mistake these walls for the city's strength," he called. "Stone can be broken. A people who refuse to kneel cannot."

The herald lowered his banner.

"Then you have chosen ruin."

Red Greave drew his sword with a whisper of steel.

"No," he answered. "I have chosen resistance. Ruin was your choice, long before you marched here."

A great horn sounded.

Arrows darkened the morning sky.

Red Greave raised his blade as shafts rattled harmlessly from the shield wall behind him.

"Archers!" he cried. "Answer them!"

The walls erupted with bowstrings.

The first ladder struck the stone below him.

Red Greave smiled as he stepped forward.

"Welcome to Blackwall," he said. "You will find its hospitality... memorable."

name: red.greave.7
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Dragon of Bleakmount, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The villagers had built the pyre before Red Greave arrived.

It stood at the foot of the mountain, stacked high with dry pine and old fear.

"You mean to burn the dragon?" he asked.

The elder shook his head.

"We mean to burn the offering."

Red Greave's eyes settled upon the young maiden bound beside the woodpile.

"Then I have come in good time."

A roar rolled down from the peaks, deep enough to stir loose stones from the cliffs.

The villagers scattered.

Red Greave alone remained where he stood.

He drew his sword and planted it in the earth before him.

"Come down," he called toward the summit. "Take issue with me today."

The dragon appeared through the clouds like a falling fortress, wings blotting out the afternoon sun.

It landed upon the ridge above the village, smoke curling between its teeth.

"Another knight," it rumbled. "Another fool."

Red Greave smiled.

"Neither. Merely a man unwilling to let monsters choose the price of peace."

The dragon lowered its great head until one golden eye filled his vision.

"And if I refuse?"

Red Greave pulled his sword free of the ground.

"Then this mountain shall remember that there are creatures more stubborn than dragons."

The beast laughed, and the cliffs answered in kind.

Then it spread its wings.

name: red.greave.8
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Judgment of Stonehaven, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The courtroom fell silent as Red Greave entered, boots echoing across the polished stone.

The magistrates sat in a semicircle above him, robed in crimson and gold, their verdict already written before a word had been spoken.

"You stand before the High Court of Stonehaven," the Chief Magistrate proclaimed. "What have you to say in your defense?"

Red Greave removed his gloves and laid them carefully upon the defendant's table.

"Nothing."

The magistrates exchanged puzzled glances.

"Nothing?"

Red Greave looked slowly around the chamber, meeting the eyes of the merchants, guards, and petitioners crowded into the gallery.

"I have not come to defend myself," he said. "I have come to defend justice."

Murmurs swept through the hall.

The Chief Magistrate struck the bench with his staff.

"Mind your words."

Red Greave smiled, though there was little warmth in it.

"I always do."

He stepped forward until he stood beneath the great scales carved into the wall.

"A court exists to weigh truth against falsehood," he declared. "Yet here I find only fear weighed against convenience."

Guards moved toward him.

Red Greave did not reach for his sword.

"Stand where you are," he said, his voice carrying effortlessly through the chamber. "I have crossed kingdoms to find justice. Do not ask me to settle for procedure."

One of the magistrates rose from his seat.

"You presume much, swordsman."

Red Greave inclined his head.

"No," he replied. "I merely expect more."

At that moment, the great doors behind him burst open.

name: red.greave.9
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Idol of the Broken Coast, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The tide was on its way out when Red Greave reached the sea caves.

The old fisherman waited beside a weathered pillar of stone, hat clutched in both hands.

"You'll not find treasure in there," he warned. "Only the thing that keeps taking those who go looking."

Red Greave looked toward the black mouth of the cave where waves breathed in and out like a sleeping beast.

"Then it has waited long enough."

He lit a lantern and stepped beneath the arch.

The cave widened into a chamber where the sea reached almost to the ceiling. At its center stood the Idol of the Broken Coast, carved from a single block of green-black stone, wrapped in chains so ancient they had become part of the rock itself.

Water dripped from its sightless eyes.

"Who binds you?" Red Greave asked.

The chamber answered with silence.

Then the chains began to move.

Links thicker than a man's arm tightened with a grinding groan, drawing the idol slowly upright. The sea churned around its base.

Red Greave drew his sword.

"Hear me," he declared, his voice echoing from the wet stone. "No prisoner is made righteous by age, and no jailer is made innocent by time."

One of the chains snapped with the crack of a ship's mast.

The idol's great head turned.

"You speak," came a voice like grinding cliffs, "as though you know which of us is the captive."

Red Greave raised his blade.

"Then enlighten me," he said. "But know this - I have never mistaken a chain for a crown."

The sea surged into the chamber as the final shackle began to break.

name: red.greave.10
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Arena of Bloodstone, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The gates opened with the groan of old timber, and the roar of twenty thousand voices poured into the arena.

Red Greave walked alone across the crimson sand.

He carried neither shield nor banner. Only his sword.

Across the arena, the Champion of Bloodstone rolled his shoulders beneath a suit of gilded armor. His spear was taller than two men, its point black as obsidian.

"You are late," the champion called.

Red Greave smiled.

"Justice keeps its own hour."

Laughter rippled through the stands.

The herald raised his staff.

"There shall be no quarter!"

The bronze gong rang.

The champion charged with astonishing speed, spear leveled like a lance.

Red Greave neither flinched nor retreated.

"A swift hand is admirable," he said as the spear flashed toward him. "A steadfast heart is indispensable."

Steel met steel with a crack that echoed around the arena.

The spear glanced aside.

Red Greave stepped inside its reach, forcing the larger man backward across the sand.

"Strength delights the crowd," he declared. "Character endures after the cheering fades."

The champion bellowed and swept the spear in a great circle.

Sand exploded into the air.

When it settled, Red Greave was already advancing.

"Rise," he said. "You have not yet shown me the measure of your resolve."

The crowd fell strangely quiet. Even they could sense the duel had only just begun.

name: red.greave.11
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Halls of the Crimson Dead, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The torches had burned for a thousand years.

Red Greave took one from its iron bracket and held it high as he crossed the threshold. The flame did not flicker. The air itself seemed unwilling to breathe.

Rows of warriors stood in silent ranks along the hall, their crimson armor dulled by centuries of dust. Each rested both hands upon the pommel of a great sword.

None moved.

"A solemn company," Red Greave observed.

His voice echoed once.

Then every helmet turned toward him.

Steel whispered from a hundred scabbards.

Red Greave drew his own blade without haste.

"Hear me," he declared. "No oath endures beyond honor. If yours has become a prison, I shall set you free."

The nearest warrior stepped from the line, crimson cloak trailing across the stone like spilled blood.

"We guard," it answered, its voice dry as old parchment.

"What do you guard?"

"We no longer remember."

Red Greave nodded sadly.

"Then your duty has outlived its purpose."

The first sword fell.

Red Greave met it cleanly, the clash ringing through the vast chamber like a cathedral bell.

One by one the remaining warriors advanced, perfectly silent.

Red Greave raised his sword.

"Come, then," he said. "Let memory end where mercy begins."

The torchlight vanished beneath a forest of crimson blades.

name: red.greave.12
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Sky Pirates of Aurelion, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The grappling hooks struck like thunder.

Iron bit into the rail, ropes snapped taut, and the merchant captain cried out as the pirate vessel hauled itself alongside.

Red Greave stepped onto the quarterdeck and looked across the shrinking gap between the ships.

"Must every journey end with theft?" he called.

A woman in a crimson coat vaulted onto the rail of the pirate ship, a curved sabre resting easily across her shoulders.

"Only the profitable ones."

Her crew laughed.

Red Greave did not.

He drew his sword in a single smooth motion.

"Profit gained without honor is merely another form of poverty."

"That is the dullest thing anyone has ever said to me."

"Then today shall be memorable in more ways than one."

The pirate captain sprang across the gap.

Red Greave met her halfway, their blades ringing together above the empty sky.

The ships lurched apart.

For a heartbeat they stood balanced on crossing swords, nothing beneath them but clouds.

"You are a long way from solid ground," the pirate captain smiled.

Red Greave answered with a smile of his own.

"Courage has never depended upon the landscape."

The ropes groaned.

Somewhere overhead, a great canvas sail tore loose in the wind.

Then the ships began to drift apart.

name: red.greave.13
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Masks of Gilded Night, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

Music echoed through the palace long after the dancers had stopped.

Red Greave stood alone beneath a thousand lanterns, his sword still sheathed as masked nobles drifted silently around the marble hall.

"A splendid celebration," he said. "It is a pity someone has mistaken it for a murder."

A woman wearing a golden fox mask inclined her head.

"You presume much, swordsman."

Red Greave smiled.

"Experience is merely presumption that has survived examination."

The orchestra began again.

Every musician wore a silver mask.

Every note sounded just a little too slow.

Red Greave's hand settled upon the pommel of his sword.

"There is still time," he called, his voice carrying effortlessly above the waltz. "Whoever has come here with treachery in his heart may yet depart with honor."

Laughter answered him from somewhere within the crowd.

A goblet shattered.

Then another.

The dancers stopped as one.

Red Greave drew his blade.

"So be it," he said. "When truth enters by the front door, deceit is always the first to flee."

Steel flashed beneath embroidered cloaks.

The lanterns swung wildly overhead as the ballroom dissolved into chaos.

name: red.greave.14
body:
You pick a book at random from the shelf: "Red Greave and the Gate of Cindered Names, by Dame Eleanor Cross"

Opening it midway, you scan a few paragaphs of the dense and cheaply printed text...

~~~~~~~~

The mountain had been hollow for longer than kingdoms had endured.

Red Greave descended the final stair and emerged into a cavern so vast that its roof was lost beyond the reach of firelight. At its center stood a gate of black stone, carved with thousands of names that glowed like embers beneath ash.

None of them were still.

They shifted slowly across the surface of the stone, trading places as though searching for someone who had not yet arrived.

Red Greave regarded the gate in silence.

"There are too many names," he said at last.

A voice answered from beyond the stone.

"There are never enough."

Flames gathered between the pillars, taking the shape of a tall figure crowned with smoke.

"You have crossed deserts and mountains to stand before me," it said. "Will you not ask what waits beyond this gate?"

Red Greave drew his sword, its polished steel reflecting a thousand burning names.

"I have found that doors seldom improve by being opened."

The figure laughed, and sparks drifted upward into the darkness.

"Every man wishes to know his destiny."

Red Greave took another step.

"A man's destiny is not found beyond a gate."

He raised the sword until its point rested upon the ancient stone.

"It is forged by the road he chooses before he reaches it."

The first crack spread across the gate like lightning through glass.

The names stopped moving.

Then every one of them began to burn.
